title: [RFC] Experimental opt-in `--cuda`, inspired by GEM-style state-batched execution
body:
Translation assistance by ChatGPT

  Hi ,
  Before opening the PR, I’d like feedback on scope and acceptance criteria for an experimental, opt-in
  `--sim-accel` sidecar path.

  This work is inspired by GEM-style ideas (GPU-oriented boolean/dataflow evaluation and state batching),
  but implemented as a Verilator-side experimental sidecar flow.
https://github.com/NVlabs/GEM

  ## Goal

  Provide a **sidecar acceleration path** for research/benchmarking:
  - opt-in only
  - no change to standard Verilator runtime semantics
  - explicit supported subset (not full coverage)

  ## Non-goals (initial step)

  - Not replacing normal Verilator runtime execution with CUDA
  - Not full AST/operator coverage
  - Not claiming universal speedup

  ## Faster test cases (measured)

  ### 1) Real module, large state batch (`cv32e40p_core`)
  Command (equivalent flow):
  ```bash
  verilator/scripts/noc_cc_direct_cuda_bench.sh \
    --verilator ./bin/verilator \
    --top-module cv32e40p_core \
    --outdir /tmp/cv32_gpu_only_example \
    --nstates 131072 \
    --gpu-reps 200 \
    --cpu-reps 20 \
    -- -f <manifest-expanded flist>
  ```
• --gpu-reps and --cpu-reps are timing loop counts, not extra test vectors.

  - --gpu-reps 200: run the GPU kernel 200 times and report average gpu_ms_per_rep
  - --cpu-reps 20: run the CPU reference 20 times and report average cpu_ms_per_rep

  Result:

  - nvars=299, nstates=131072
  - gpu_ms_per_rep=0.104023
  - cpu_ms_per_rep=71.704341
  - speedup_cpu_over_gpu=689.312116x
  - mismatch=0
  - cuda_assignw_supported=90, cuda_assignw_total=479, coverage=30.06%

  ### 2) CV32 E2E proxy flow (coremark + same RTL set)

  Result snapshot:

  - gpu_ms_per_rep=0.015232
  - cpu_ms_per_rep=15.391155
  - speedup_cpu_over_gpu=1010.448722x
  - mismatch=0
  - coverage=30.06%

  ## Also measured (for balance)

  Small regression case (t_sim_accel_bench_exec) is GPU-unfavorable:

  - nvars=7, nstates=8192
  - gpu_ms_per_rep=0.009972
  - cpu_ms_per_rep=0.006530
  - speedup_cpu_over_gpu=0.654808x
  - mismatch=0
  - coverage=100.00%

  So performance is strongly dependent on batching size (nstates), coverage, and design structure.

  ## Correctness gates used

  - CPU-vs-GPU output comparison in bench flow
  - pass condition requires mismatch=0
  - unsupported subset is reported explicitly (no silent “full acceleration” claim)

  ## Environment

  - Branch: feature/sim-accel-pr-clean-v2
  - Commit: 672eae32a97af3125a9555b3e5ad5f545d19f573
  - OS: Ubuntu 24.04.2 LTS (WSL2)
  - CPU: Intel Core i7-14700F (14C/28T)
  - GPU: NVIDIA GeForce RTX 4070 Ti SUPER (16GB)
  - Driver: 566.36
  - CUDA toolkit: nvcc 12.0 (runtime shown by nvidia-smi: CUDA 12.7)

  ## Feedback requested

  1. Is this acceptable as an initial opt-in sidecar-only step?
  2. Is the sim-accel naming (backend / strategy) acceptable?
  3. Are these correctness gates sufficient for an initial PR?
  4. Preferred benchmark/reporting format for review?
